html:
Introduction: What Makes Jamaica Payroll Different

Running payroll in Jamaica is more complex than many employers expect. Unlike some countries with a single payroll tax, Jamaica has five separate statutory deductions that every employer must calculate, track, and remit correctly: PAYE income tax, National Insurance Scheme (NIS) contributions, National Housing Trust (NHT) deductions, Education Tax, and the Heart Trust/NTA levy.

Each has different rates, different calculation bases, different remittance portals, and different deadlines. A mistake on any one of them can result in penalties from Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ), the NHT, or the Ministry of Labour.

This guide walks through every step of the Jamaica payroll process — from registering as an employer through to annual filings — so that you understand exactly what's required and how to get it right.

Step 1: Register as an Employer in Jamaica

Before you pay your first employee, you need to be registered with the correct authorities. Trying to run payroll without registration is the fastest path to compliance problems.

Tax Registration Number (TRN)

Your business needs a TRN from Tax Administration Jamaica before registering for PAYE. If you don't have a business TRN, apply at any TAJ office or online at the TAJ portal. Sole traders use their personal TRN; companies need a separate business TRN.

PAYE Registration with TAJ

Register your business as a PAYE employer at etax.gov.jm or in person at a TAJ office. You'll receive a PAYE file number — keep this, you need it for every monthly remittance.

Required documents:

  • Business TRN
  • Certificate of Incorporation (for companies) or business registration documents
  • Proof of business address
  • Expected number of employees and anticipated monthly payroll

NIS Employer Registration

Register with the National Insurance Scheme at the Ministry of Labour and Social Security. You'll receive an NIS employer registration number. Monthly NIS contributions must be remitted using this number.

NHT Employer Registration

Register with the National Housing Trust at their employer portal. You'll receive an NHT employer registration number — separate from your PAYE file number and NIS registration.

Registering Each Employee

Before processing any employee's payroll, you need:

  • Their TRN (Tax Registration Number)
  • Their NIS registration number — if they don't have one, they must register before their first pay period
  • Their NHT beneficiary number — separate from TRN and NIS number
  • A TD4 form from their previous employer if they were employed elsewhere in the same tax year (April 1–March 31)

Step 2: Understand Jamaica's Payroll Deductions

Jamaica payroll involves five statutory deductions. You need to understand all five to calculate any payslip correctly.

1. PAYE — Pay As You Earn Income Tax

PAYE is income tax deducted at source from employees' wages on behalf of TAJ.

  • Calculated on: Taxable income (gross pay minus personal allowance threshold)
  • Annual income tax threshold (2025/26): J$1,500,096 — income below this level is not subject to PAYE
  • Rate: 25% on taxable income up to J$6,000,000 annually; 30% on the portion above J$6,000,000
  • Employer contribution: None — PAYE is employee-side only. The employer is the collection agent for TAJ.
  • Remittance deadline: 14th of the following month (monthly payroll). For employers with employees paid weekly or bi-weekly, remittance is also monthly — due 14th of the month following the pay periods.

How to Calculate PAYE Monthly

For an employee earning J$250,000 gross per month (J$3,000,000 annually):

  1. Annual gross: J$3,000,000
  2. Minus income tax threshold: J$3,000,000 − J$1,500,096 = J$1,499,904 taxable annually
  3. PAYE at 25%: J$1,499,904 × 25% = J$374,976 annually
  4. Monthly PAYE deduction: J$374,976 ÷ 12 = J$31,248 per month

Use the PayrollJamaica calculator to verify calculations — manual errors here are common and costly.

2. NIS — National Insurance Scheme

  • Employee contribution: 3% of insurable earnings
  • Employer contribution: 3% of insurable earnings
  • Calculated on: Insurable earnings (gross wages up to the NIS insurable earnings ceiling — confirm current ceiling for 2025/26 at the Ministry of Labour)
  • Remittance deadline: 14th of the following month

Note: NIS is calculated on insurable earnings, not gross pay. If an employee earns above the insurable earnings ceiling, NIS contributions only apply to earnings up to the ceiling, not the excess.

3. NHT — National Housing Trust

  • Employee contribution: 2% of gross pay
  • Employer contribution: 3% of gross pay
  • Calculated on: Gross pay (no ceiling — unlike NIS, NHT applies to all gross earnings)
  • Remittance deadline: 14th of the following month

NHT contributions build toward employees' eligibility for NHT housing benefits. Every employee for whom you deduct NHT contributions should receive an NHT beneficiary number so their contributions are properly credited.

4. Education Tax

  • Employee contribution: 2.25% of gross pay
  • Employer contribution: 3.5% of gross pay
  • Calculated on: Gross pay
  • Remittance: Combined with the PAYE monthly remittance to TAJ

Education Tax is remitted to TAJ alongside PAYE — it's a single combined remittance, not a separate filing. However, you must report the Education Tax component separately on your TAJ remittance schedule.

5. Heart Trust/NTA Levy

  • Employee contribution: None — employer only
  • Employer contribution: 3% of gross pay
  • Calculated on: Gross pay
  • Remittance: Also combined with the monthly TAJ remittance

Heart Trust/NTA (National Training Agency) is an employer-only levy. Employees do not contribute. It funds Jamaica's vocational training programs.

Step 3: Calculate Total Payroll Cost Per Employee

Understanding the total employer cost — not just the salary — is essential for budgeting and pricing. Here's a full example for an employee earning J$150,000 gross per month:

Component Employee Pays Employer Pays Notes
Gross Salary — J$150,000 Agreed pay
PAYE Income Tax ~J$3,125 — Employee deduction, employer remits
NIS (employee 3%) ~J$4,500 — Based on insurable earnings
NIS (employer 3%) — ~J$4,500 Employer additional cost
NHT (employee 2%) J$3,000 — Employee deduction
NHT (employer 3%) — J$4,500 Employer additional cost
Education Tax (employee 2.25%) J$3,375 — Employee deduction
Education Tax (employer 3.5%) — J$5,250 Employer additional cost
Heart Trust/NTA (employer 3%) — J$4,500 Employer only
Net Pay to Employee ~J$136,000 — Gross minus all employee deductions
Total Employer Cost — ~J$168,750 Salary + all employer contributions

PAYE calculated on annual basis; monthly figure is approximate. NIS calculated assuming salary is below insurable earnings ceiling. Exact figures vary — use the PayrollJamaica calculator for your specific situation.

The gap between gross salary and total employer cost — approximately 12.5% at this income level — is the "employer on-cost" that many businesses don't budget for properly when hiring.

Step 4: Prepare Payslips

Every employee must receive a payslip showing their gross pay, all deductions, and net pay. While there is no specific legal format mandated by Jamaican law, best practice payslips include:

  • Employee name and TRN
  • Pay period (dates covered)
  • Gross pay (regular + any overtime or allowances)
  • PAYE deducted
  • NIS deducted (employee portion)
  • NHT deducted (employee portion)
  • Education Tax deducted (employee portion)
  • Any other deductions (loan repayments, etc.)
  • Net pay
  • Year-to-date totals for each deduction

Year-to-date totals are critical. Without them, you cannot prepare accurate P6 forms at year-end, and employees cannot file personal income tax returns correctly.

Step 5: Monthly Remittances to TAJ, NHT, and NIS

Calculating payroll is only half the job. You must remit the deducted amounts to the correct authorities every month.

TAJ Monthly Remittance (PAYE + Education Tax + Heart Trust)

Deadline: 14th of the following month (if the 14th falls on a weekend or public holiday, the deadline is the preceding business day)

Portal: etax.gov.jm

What to remit: Total PAYE deducted from all employees, plus employee Education Tax, plus employer Education Tax, plus employer Heart Trust/NTA levy

Form: Monthly S01 Remittance Schedule — break down by employee

Payment methods: eTAJ portal online payment, any major Jamaican bank (NCB, Scotiabank, Sagicor, JN Bank, JMMB) via bill payment using your PAYE file number

NHT Monthly Remittance

Deadline: 14th of the following month

Portal: NHT Employer Portal (employer.nht.gov.jm)

What to remit: Employee NHT (2%) + Employer NHT (3%) = 5% of gross payroll total

Important: NHT contributions must be filed by employee, not just as a lump sum — each employee's NHT contribution must be credited to their individual NHT account. This is how employees build eligibility for NHT benefits.

NIS Monthly Remittance

Deadline: 14th of the following month

Portal/Process: Ministry of Labour and Social Security NIS portal, or via approved banks

What to remit: Employee NIS (3%) + Employer NIS (3%) = 6% of insurable earnings

Consequences of Late Remittances

  • PAYE: 10% late penalty immediately, plus 20% surcharge on unpaid balance, plus interest at 16.5% per annum
  • NHT: Interest and surcharges on late contributions
  • NIS: Interest and potential legal action for persistent non-payment

The 14th deadline is not negotiable. Set a monthly calendar reminder for the 10th of each month — that gives you four days buffer to handle any banking delays or portal issues.

Step 6: Year-End Payroll Filings (Due March 31)

Jamaica's payroll year ends March 31. Every employer must complete the following by March 31 each year:

P6 Forms — Annual Employee Tax Certificates

P6 forms are the Jamaican equivalent of the US W-2. Every employee who worked for you during the tax year must receive a P6 showing their total gross earnings and all statutory deductions for the full year (April 1–March 31).

  • Employees need P6s to file their personal income tax returns
  • If an employee received income from more than one employer during the year, they need a P6 from each
  • P6 forms must be distributed to employees by March 31

SO1 Employer Annual Return — TAJ

The SO1 is the employer's annual summary to TAJ. It lists every employee who worked for you during the tax year, their total annual gross pay, and all PAYE deducted. TAJ uses this to reconcile against employees' personal tax filings.

  • Filed electronically via etax.gov.jm
  • Deadline: March 31
  • Late filing penalty: significant — file even if figures need correction later

NHT Annual Return

The NHT Annual Return summarizes all NHT contributions made on behalf of your employees for the year. This is separate from the SO1 — filing the SO1 does not satisfy this requirement.

NIS Annual Reconciliation

The NIS Annual Reconciliation confirms that all monthly NIS contributions have been made and accurately reflects each employee's contribution record for the year.

Step 7: Handling Special Situations

New Employees Hired Mid-Year

If you hire someone in, say, October, you need their TD4 from their previous employer (if they had one). The TD4 shows their year-to-date income and PAYE deducted from April to the date they left their last job. Without this, you cannot calculate their correct PAYE going forward — you risk under-deducting and leaving the employee with a personal tax liability at year-end.

Employees Who Leave During the Year

When an employee resigns or is terminated, you must:

  1. Process a final payslip covering all owed wages including any unused vacation payout
  2. Provide a TD4 form showing their earnings and deductions while employed with you
  3. Remove them from your active payroll (but retain their records for 7 years)

Overtime Pay

Under Jamaica's Employment (Termination and Redundancy Payments) Act and Labour Relations Code, overtime for most employees must be paid at 1.5x the regular rate for hours above the agreed work week. Overtime is subject to all the same statutory deductions as regular pay — PAYE, NIS, NHT, Education Tax.

Maternity and Sick Leave

Statutory maternity leave in Jamaica is 12 weeks paid at full salary. NIS provides a maternity benefit that partially offsets the employer cost — but the employer is responsible for the full payment upfront and then claims the NIS reimbursement. Payroll statutory deductions continue normally during maternity leave based on the salary paid.

Jamaica Payroll Deadlines Cheat Sheet

Deadline Action Required Authority
14th of each month PAYE + Education Tax + Heart Trust remittance (S01 schedule) TAJ
14th of each month NHT monthly contributions remittance NHT
14th of each month NIS monthly contributions remittance Ministry of Labour
March 31 annually P6 forms issued to all employees TAJ
March 31 annually SO1 Employer Annual Return filed with TAJ TAJ
March 31 annually NHT Annual Return filed NHT
March 31 annually NIS Annual Reconciliation filed Ministry of Labour

Simplifying Jamaica Payroll with Software

Every step in this guide can be done manually — spreadsheets, manual bank transfers, and manually completing TAJ portal forms. Many Jamaican business owners have done it this way for years.

But manual payroll carries constant risk: outdated rates, formula errors, missed deadlines, and the time cost of doing it all yourself.

PayrollJamaica automates all of it. When you enter employee hours or confirm a salaried pay period:

  • PAYE, NIS, NHT, Education Tax, and Heart Trust are calculated automatically using current 2025/26 rates
  • Payslips are generated and can be sent directly to employees
  • Monthly remittance schedules (TAJ S01, NHT, NIS) are generated ready to file
  • Year-to-date totals are tracked automatically for P6 and SO1 preparation

For a Jamaican business with 1–30 employees, the time savings alone — typically 3–5 hours per pay cycle — justify the cost. The penalty risk elimination is extra.
